title: Making creamed honey
subtitle: How to produce smooth, spreadable soft-set honey using the seed method.
excerpt: Creamed honey granulates to a fine, even texture that spreads like butter. The seed method controls granulation by seeding liquid honey with a small proportion of finely crystallised honey. Temperature management and patience do the rest. The result is a consistent product that stores for longer than liquid honey without setting hard.
sourceNotes: Elton Dyce, Fermentation of Honey (1931), Cornell University: original description of the Dyce process for creamed honey. BBKA honey presentation and judging notes. National Bee Unit guide to honey processing and packaging (2023).

glossaryTerms.definition: Finely crystallised honey used to inoculate a batch of liquid honey during the creaming process. The seed's crystal structure acts as a template for the new batch, encouraging rapid, fine-grained crystallisation throughout.

The natural process by which the glucose in honey precipitates into crystals, causing the honey to solidify. Crystal size determines texture: large crystals produce a gritty set; fine crystals produce a smooth, creamy result.

The balance of these two sugars in a honey determines how fast it granulates. High-glucose honeys (oilseed rape, clover) granulate quickly and are most suited to creaming. High-fructose honeys (acacia, heather) may stay liquid for months.

content.text: Honey that granulates on its own does so unpredictably: sometimes coarse and gritty, sometimes smooth, depending on temperature and the speed of the process. Creaming takes control of that process. By seeding liquid honey with a proportion of finely crystallised
seed honey
and then holding the batch at the right temperature, the beekeeper produces a consistently smooth set. The
granulation
that results is fine-grained throughout.
Which honey works best
Honey with a high glucose content granulates readily and takes to the seed method quickly. Oilseed rape and clover honeys are the standard UK examples. A low
glucose to fructose ratio
in varieties such as acacia or heather means slow or no granulation; these are harder to cream and are better sold as liquid.
Sourcing seed honey

The seed must have a fine, even crystal structure with no large granules. Commercial creamed honey with a smooth, paste-like texture is a reliable source. Check the label: it should state no additives. Alternatively, keep a small amount of the previous year's finely creamed honey back as seed for the next batch. Seed honey that has been stored well in a cool cupboard retains its crystal structure for months.
The process
1. Warm the liquid honey
If your freshly extracted honey has already started to granulate coarsely, warm it in a water bath at 40 C to re-liquefy it completely. Allow it to cool to below 25 C before adding the seed.
2. Add the seed

The classic Dyce ratio is 10% seed by weight: 100 g of seed per 1 kg of liquid honey. Add the seed to the warm liquid honey and stir slowly and thoroughly with a clean spoon or paddle, working from the bottom of the bucket upward. Avoid whipping in air, which causes foam on the surface.
3. Jar immediately
Pour or spoon the seeded honey into clean jars straight after mixing while it is still liquid enough to pour. Leave 1 cm of headroom. Seal each jar.
4. Set in a cool room

Move the jars to a room held between 14 C and 18 C. At this temperature granulation happens over 1 to 2 weeks and the crystals are fine. Above 21 C the process slows or stops; below 10 C the honey sets very hard. A garage or unheated pantry through autumn is the natural environment.

The honey has reached the right point when a spoon pressed into the surface leaves a clean impression that holds its shape. The texture should be smooth with no grittiness between the fingers. If it feels gritty, the seed honey itself had large crystals, or the liquid honey was seeded while still warm enough to dissolve the fine crystal structure.
Storage and shelf life

Store creamed honey in a cool cupboard away from direct heat. It keeps well for 12 to 18 months if the moisture content of the original honey was below 20%. Avoid the fridge: the cold temperature causes excessive hardening and affects spreading quality. If a jar softens at room temperature in summer, this is normal; it is still safe to eat.
Honey refuses to set in the jars
Room temperature too high, honey variety unsuitable for creaming, or insufficient seed ratio.
Move jars to a cooler room. If the honey is predominantly acacia or heather, it may not cream reliably; blend it with a quick-granulating clover or oilseed rape honey at 50:50 before reseeding.
Gritty texture in the finished product
Seed honey had large crystals, or the liquid honey was still above 25 C when the seed was added, dissolving the fine crystal structure.
Source finer seed honey with a paste-like consistency. Allow the liquid honey to cool fully before seeding next time.
